data.title: The War Premium Vs. The Yield Premium: Which Force Will Seize Control And Drive Gold's Next Move?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices experienced a rollercoaster start to the week with Hormuz inflation risks front and center after the US and Iran failed to reach a deal in Pakistan over the weekend.

The precious metal continues to struggle for a clear direction as two opposing forces, namely, the rising Middle East tensions and a hawkish shift in the Fed's outlook, tug at the precious metal.

Despite a recovery since the initial post-war selloff, the "safe-haven" rally appears to be losing its legs as traders weigh the risk of a regional conflict against the reality of "higher-for-longer" US interest rates.

The Geopolitical Floor: Hormuz and the Iran Deadline

The primary driver keeping gold from a deeper retracement is the simmering tension between the US and Iran. With the deadline for US-Iran mediation approaching, the market remains on edge.

Reports of a potential blockade of the Strait of Hormuz have sent shockwaves through the commodities sector, primarily boosting oil prices but also providing a fundamental "ceiling" for gold as markets fear the higher-for-longer rates narrative.

The downside is limited by the hopes of a potential deal, given President Donald Trump's record of "TACO". This is keeping the precious metal supported for now and sort of in a state of limbo until some clarity on the US-Iran situation comes to fruition.

The Macro Headwind: Fed Outlook and a Resilient Dollar

The Federal Reserve continues to act as the primary headwind. Recent US economic data has been uncomfortably resilient, forcing markets to rethink the timing and depth of rate cuts for 2026. This hawkish repricing has breathed new life into the US dollar and pushed Treasury yields higher, making non-yielding assets like gold less attractive.

From a macro perspective, the "war premium" is currently being offset by the "yield premium." As long as the US economy continues to defy gravity, the Fed has little reason to pivot, and that remains the single biggest hurdle for gold's path toward the psychological $5,000 mark.

Key Takeaways

Given the factors discussed above, gold is in a state of limbo, and this looks set to continue. The only way the current narrative shifts is with some sort of resolution regarding the Iran conflict.

If mediation fails and the Hormuz blockade becomes a reality, we could see a vertical move as the market prices in a massive inflationary shock, and higher rates could send gold prices tumbling.

Conversely, if a deal is struck, expect the "fear premium" to evaporate quickly, but gold could benefit as markets may view the inflation situation to be (for lack of a better word) transitory.

For now, gold is a passenger to the headlines. Traders should keep a close eye on the US Dollar Index (DXY) and energy prices, as these will be the primary leading indicators for the next major leg in the yellow metal.

Technical Outlook - Gold

Looking at the charts, gold's recovery is entering its third week, but the momentum is visibly waning. The price is oscillating within a tight range, finding immediate support at the $4,700 psychological level.

While the 100-period SMA (blue) has transitioned from resistance to dynamic support near $4,606, the upside remains capped by the descending 200-period SMA (orange) currently hovering around $4,870.

The RSI sits neutrally at 51.84, confirming a lack of directional momentum. Bulls need a decisive break above the recent swing high at $4,804 to challenge the $4,870 SMA and the elusive $5,000 handle. Conversely, a breach of the $4,700 support could see a swift retracement toward the $4,600 confluence zone.

Key Levels to Watch:

Resistance: $4,804, $4,870, $5,000

Support: $4,700, $4,606, $4,500

Gold (XAU/USD) One-Hour Chart, April 13, 2026

data.title: Look At The Starz, Look How They Shine For You
data.description:
Starz (STRZ) is a cable/streaming channel that focuses on premium programming for women. About 70% of subscribers are women, with much programming like Power and Outlander targeting a female audience. I believe the stock trades at an extremely compelling valuation with several upside drivers, including a potential buyout.

STRZ has about 17.6 million US subscribers. As you can see below, the OTT (streaming) subs have been growing at a nice clip while the linear subs have been declining. OTT is now about 72% of the total US subs, and grew 7.5% year over year and 3% sequentially. Linear is ~28% of subs and declined about 16%. The nice news about that math is that we are nearing an inflection point where OTT is at the size where its lower percent growth rate can overcome linear's higher percent growth rate in absolute terms.

The company reorganized their Canadian operations into a pure licensing arrangement that gets reflected on the linear and other revenue lines on the income statement. It's a small part of the business, and that move allows management to focus on US operations.

Revenue has been split at almost the same ratio as subscribers. In the nine months ended Dec 31, revenue was about 68% streaming vs. the rest linear and other (other includes Canada).

The company does not break out OIBDA (EBITDA with stock comp added back) by source (OTT vs. linear), and quarterly results have been pre-split, and immediately post-split have some items that won't appear going forward. However, this most recent quarter was largely clean, and EBITDA came in at $55.5 million.

You cannot completely annualize the December quarter results because of the timing of expenses and promotions, but the $204 million of OIBDA for 2025 slightly exceeded the company's $200 million guidance. Moreover, by producing its own shows, it reduces the programming amortization line item (effectively the cost of licensing the content from Lionsgate). The company believes that a combination of subscriber/revenue growth and this lower expense from in-house production can increase OIBDA from the $200 million range in 2025 to $300 million in 2028.

Performance as a Separate Entity

STRZ stock has underperformed since the separation from LION for what I believe are a few reasons:

  • It is a small cap (under $250 million market value) with limited liquidity, which limits the pool of potential investors.
    • The float is even smaller, with the two top holders owning about 28% of the shares. More on that below.
  • It is viewed more as a linear channel than a streaming service despite the subscriber data stated above. Thus it is viewed as a melting ice cube.
  • While STRZ has multiyear contracts for its hit shows, Lionsgate owns the content library. Many investors only want services that own the IP.
  • Limited time as a standalone company. Spinoffs and corporate separations often need time to gain traction as separate entities.

I think these items are largely why STRZ trades cheap on a relative and absolute basis, and I will tackle them one by one.

Small Cap

That's a bit of a chicken and egg issue. Part of why the stock is cheap is because it's a small cap, but part of why it's a small cap is because it's cheap. While it may seem counterintuitive, some investors wait until companies are higher valued/higher market caps before they play. The trick is to have the capital/disposition where you can tolerate the volatility that comes with lower liquidity/smaller cap shares.

On the large holder front, Steve Mnuchin just sold his 10.7% stake for $13.85/share to Byron Allen, who owns several media properties, including the Weather Channel. Allen has had mixed results as a buyer and owner of media assets. What matters, though, is that I have not seen a situation where Allen owns passive, minority stakes in anything. Only whole companies. There were two notable developments that occurred after this transaction. First, Allen filed a 13D, which implies he is not a passive investor. Second, in response to Allen's 13D, the company put a poison pill in place. LION put a poison pill in place when it first started trading as a separate company. The stated goal of that pill was "to protect shareholder interests, ensure fair value in any change-of-control transaction, and uphold the board's fiduciary duties." This is standard language to force a hostile bidder to negotiate rather than say the company is not for sale. I think it's reasonable to believe that STRZ's board has similar goals.

Linear vs. Streaming

STRZ is the only example I can think of that has transitioned the bulk of its subs from linear to streaming. As you can see in that table above, this dynamic continues. The company gained 370k net streaming subscribers this past quarter. The slate appears good to keep current subscribers happy and sign up new ones. The Spartacus revamp has been well received. The last season of Outlander just premiered. The Outlander spin-off, Blood of My Blood, premiered last summer. Its sequel will come later this year, as will Fightland, the STRZ's first series it produced on its own in partnership with 50 Cent. I think the melting ice cube misperception will dissipate over time as revenue and subscribers grow, which, most importantly, will grow profits.

Content Library

Fightland hopefully doesn't just mean a higher margin. It can also ease the concern around the lack of a library. This concern is not just with equity investors. Even though the company finished December with 2.9x net debt to OIBDA, which normally translates to at least a BBB rating, the debt ratings agencies were going to give the company's debt a CCC rating (low junk) because in the event of something like a global hack where subscribers could not access the service for an extended period of time and dropped their subscriptions, lack of library meant low recovery. The company chose to go unrated and expects to finish 2026 with 2.7x net leverage. For those of you who can buy the STRZ 2029 bonds, I think they're an excellent risk reward in the mid-80s.

Limited Time as Public Company

One can't make time move faster. I do think that most shareholders who didn't want to own STRZ shares post the separation have had the opportunity to sell.

Valuation

Here is where I get excited (and perhaps what caught Byron Allen's attention). STRZ is cheap on a relative and absolute basis.

Market Cap (@$14.57) $244 million
Debt $625 million
Cash $35 million
Enterprise Value $834 million
EV/2025 OIBDA ($204mm) 4.08x
EV/Run Rate OIBDA ($222mm) 3.76x
Unlevered FCF Yield ($60mm midpt) 24.5%

These numbers compare favorably to AMC Networks, the only other independent streaming service and linear channel. AMC has more revenue than STRZ but reports only 10.5 million streaming subscribers. It does not report linear subs. AMC has a small IP library. It owns the Walking Dead franchise as well as rights to Anne Rice's literary works (Interview with a Vampire, etc.) and a few others like Gangs of London. Other than Walking Dead, none of those series strike me as major hits with major followings.

Yet, despite fewer OTT subs, more debt (~5x leverage), and just a tiny library, AMC trades at around 5x EBITDA. If STRZ were to match this multiple on just 2025 OIBDA, the stock could more than double.

In addition, on a absolute basis, I consider any company that is growing and still trading around 4x EBITDA and above a 20% FCF yield to be extremely cheap on an absolute basis. My guess is Byron Allen agrees with me.

Risk

The main risk here (beyond a global hack that leads to massive subscriber losses) is that subscribers, particularly OTT subs, start bleeding. That can happen from subscribers turning against the content, the service not pricing right, or a number of other reasons. The other risk is Fightland is a major flop. In that scenario, the company is out the cost of production and has a hole in its content.

Conclusion

I think STRZ is a unique asset that trades cheap on a relative and absolute basis and has multiple catalysts to create value. The fastest would be a bid from Byron Allen to buy the whole company. Should that not materialize, free cash flow generation and building a content library of its own can create enormous value in the intermediate term. I think the shares can easily double from here with room for more gains.

data.title: One Streak Ends, Others Make History
data.description:
Over the weekend, ceasefire talks fell apart, and the Strait of Hormuz started facing a blockade this morning. Despite these negative catalysts leading to a negative tone at Monday's open, equities rallied throughout the session, with the S&P 500 finishing up over 1%.

That return to green candles also meant the S&P 500 has now rallied in eight of the last nine sessions. In fact, Friday was the only down day recently, and that snapped a seven-day winning streak.

As shown below, that was the longest streak of consecutive up days since another seven days last October. Going back to 2000, that was the 29th example of a winning streak of 7 days or more, with the longest going for nine days last May.

Again, winning streaks lasting at least 7 days have been somewhat uncommon. Filtering out those streaks to only look at those without another one in the prior six months, as was the case recently, there have been 48 instances since the start of the five-day trading week in 1953.

As shown below, from the close of the first down day that ended those winning streaks, the S&P 500's average performance has been modestly stronger than the norm and consistently positive in the weeks and months ahead.

The broad index isn't alone in stringing together a number of up days in a row. Of all S&P 500 members, seven are currently on even longer winning streaks of at least nine trading days, with one stock, State Street (STT), now on a ten-day winning streak.

In the charts below, we show how those streaks stack up versus their respective histories. None of these winning streaks has gone on long enough to be records, although STT and Microchip (MCHP) are each only one day away.

We would note that most of these stocks, other than STT, are in the tech sector or adjacent to it. Regarding Intel (INTC), its winning streak is one of many that have lasted 9 days throughout its history, but as we discussed in an earlier post, there hasn't been a time since 1984 that it has risen by more in such a span.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Existing Home Sales Remained Sluggish In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Existing home sales retreated in March, falling 3.6% following a brief rebound in February. According to the National Association of Realtors (NAR), sales settled at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 3.98 million units, the lowest level since last June and trailing the projected 4.07 million. On a year-over-year basis, sales are down 1.0%.

Insights from the NAR

Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun pointed to a "perfect storm" of headwinds currently stalling the market: buyer hesitancy, the inventory gap, and price resilience.

“March home sales remained sluggish and below last year’s pace,” said NAR Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun. “Lower consumer confidence and softer job growth continue to hold back buyers.”

“Inventory remains a major constraint on the market,” Yun said. “The inventory-to-sales ratio, or supply-to-demand ratio, is below historical norms. An additional 300,000 to 500,000 homes for sale would help bring the market closer to normal conditions and allow consumers to make purchase decisions without feeling rushed.”

“Because inventory remains limited, the median home price rose to a new record high for the month of March,” Yun added. “That price growth has helped the typical homeowner accumulate $128,100 in housing wealth over the past six years.”

(Press release)

Background: Why Existing Home Sales Matter

Existing home sales measures the monthly sales of previously owned single-family homes, condos, and co-ops. Because they account for roughly 90% of the residential market, they serve as a vital pulse check on the broader economy.

There is a high correlation between these sales and broader consumer spending. A move into a "new" existing home typically triggers secondary purchases in furnishings, appliances, and home improvement. Conversely, a sustained downturn in this data often serves as a leading indicator of economic contraction.

Over this time frame, we clearly see the real estate bubble, which peaked in 2005 and then fell dramatically. Sales were volatile for the first year or so following the Great Recession, with monthly sales as low as 3.45 million units and as high as 5.44 million units. We have seen that same volatility following the most recent recession, with sales ranging from 3.85 million units to 6.73 million units.

Existing Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

While raw data shows a market in flux, adjusting for population growth reveals a deeper trend. Since 2000, the U.S. population has grown by approximately 23.3%. Existing home sales are 23.9% below the NAR's January 2000 estimate, but when we account for population growth, current sales levels are 37.6% below turn-of-the-century levels.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed rate mortgage in March 2026 was 6.18%. While lower than recent peaks, the rate remains high enough to sustain the "lock-in effect," where homeowners are reluctant to trade in their current low-interest mortgages for new, higher-rate loans, further suppressing available inventory.

Existing Home Sales: Median Price

Despite the decline in sales volume, limited inventory continues to drive costs higher. The median price for an existing home rose for the second consecutive month in March, reaching a record high for the month of $408,800.

This represents a 2.7% increase from the previous month and a 1.4% increase from one year ago, marking the 33rd consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor Q1: Middle East War Consequences And The Zero-Availability Crisis
data.description:
Introduction

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) has earnings right around the corner, so I'll use this chance to initiate coverage of this company on Seeking Alpha. Most of my analyses are about the technology behind modern innovations, like robotics and AI, so of course, my attention would naturally be drawn to everything that makes this innovation possible, including chips and memory. There are other areas, of course, like optics, with Marvell (MRVL) for example, energy with Exxon Mobil (XOM), cooling with Vertiv Holdings (VRT), etc., that could be discussed, but today I want to break down TSMC for its central position not only in the AI and data center boom but also in the memory shortage.

TSMC Then and Now

TSMC is probably the best example of the industry turnaround as a result of AI adoption. Before the data-center rapid buildout had started, TSMC had Apple (AAPL) as the biggest customer, and was primarily focused on the smartphone and mobile device market. It produced chips for phones and computers, also serving companies like Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) for gaming console chips. As you can imagine, they were seeing revenues way smaller than they do right now, and it's not surprising, as the growth there has never been as explosive, except during the pandemic, when the market became overly stimulated and the demand for mobile devices and computers soared. This industry, in general, had always been cyclical, so after the major revenue expansion came a pretty sharp revenue decline, as can be seen on the graph below:

In the middle of 2024, the growth not only returned, but also reaccelerated to levels the company has never seen. It's not driven by mobile devices (sales of which are mostly flat except for Apple), but by the beginning of the data center boom. Although the mobile segment also contributed. I still believe, though, that the cycles will most likely remain, maybe in a different form.

Today, TSMC revenue growth is at around 30% YoY, but it's projected to slow to the mid and low twenties in the next two years, reaching the cycle bottom in 2028-2029 with the growth of only 6%, and then continuing the growth at a 20%-plus rate. A couple of notes I'd like to make here. First, this is an incredibly stable company, and later in this article, you'll see additional metrics that confirm this statement. A slight decrease in revenue growth every year for the next three years sounds like a reasonable assumption, but the revenue growth of 20%-plus for an industry leader, which is effectively a monopoly, is still incredible growth. If you accept this assumption, it means you agree that the industry will continue exploding.

This also would most likely mean that the capex fears won't materialize and the data center buildout will remain almost at the same speed as it is right now. I'm not saying it's impossible, and given the fact that both TSMC and all of its hyperscaler customers increase the capex spending in 2026, in some ways, we can already say that the fears didn't come true, but it's something every investor should keep in mind before buying stocks of an AI-related boom. If you believe the market is correct, then stocks like TSM aren't the best investment for you at this moment. At the same time, the data center buildout is evolving. In 2023-2024, computing was directed into AI training with special chips suitable for this task, but now there's a surge in AI inference demand (for using trained models), which is projected to continue growing as there are more and more AI usage cases. Training requires way more computing and stronger chips, but the end product, or a model, is being used by way more machines, making the demand for inference infrastructure soar.

Q4 2025

Reading the transcript from earnings, I noticed not only the investors who were scared of the AI-bubble, but also the CEO of TSMC, who took the topic very seriously:

I spend a lot of time in the last three or four months talking to my customers and end customers' customer. I want to make sure that my customers demand are real. So I talked to those cloud service providers, all of them. The answer is that I'm quite satisfied with the answer. Actually, they show me the evidence that the AI really help their business. So they grow their business successfully and healthy in their financial return. - C.C. Wei, CEO

That, of course, doesn't mean that the demand is here to stay, but I believe he can see way more than we do, and I trust the judgment of the CEO of one of the best world manufacturers.

Talking about the numbers, TSMC beat on everything. It had more than $33 billion in revenue in the final quarter. It's incredible to see that advanced chips (7nm and below) now account for 75%-plus of total wafer revenue.

Management is humble and grounded, even though the numbers are huge, but they still realize that silicon is still the biggest bottleneck, and they're correct. We can see the confirmation of the fact from its partners, like Broadcom (AVGO):

A Broadcom executive said its manufacturing partner Taiwan Semiconductor is reaching capacity limits and the industry is facing broader supply chain constraints amid surging demand for AI chips, Reuters reported.

They're running at full capacity and are building out additional manufacturing facilities mostly outside of Taiwan to minimize the geopolitical risks. While their gross margins expanded this quarter to a staggering 62.3%, which is unheard of for a manufacturer, the company guides for a margin squeeze from those overseas facilities:

Compared to third quarter, our fourth quarter gross margin increased by 280 basis points sequentially to 62.3%, primarily due to cost improvement efforts, a more favorable foreign exchange rate and a higher overall capacity utilization rate.....

As the scale of our overseas expansion grows, we continue to forecast the gross margin dilution from the ramp-up of overseas fabs in the next several years to be between 2% to 3% in the early stages and widen to 3% to 4% in the latter stages.

This is not critical, as even a gross margin in the high fifties would make them a top-performing manufacturer, and in my opinion, as long as it's a price for diversifying away from under the Chinese loom, it's entirely worth it.

Looking ahead to the upcoming earnings report, the guidance is incredibly aggressive. They’re projecting Q1 revenue to be between $34.6 billion and $35.8 billion, which would be a nearly 40% jump year-over-year.

The main story for 2026 is the new 2-nanometer (N2) node, which just got to mass production during Q4 2025. I really like how the company doesn't stop at where it gets - 2-nanometer is an incredible advancement in technology, but they are already looking toward the A16 family, which is the next gen coming in 2027-2028.

They're planning to spend between $52 billion and $56 billion on capex this year, which is an astronomical sum even for this company, but to be fair, they are at capacity already today. If they could take those Arizona and Japan fabs online now, they would sell them out; it would not stand empty, so for now it looks entirely justified. C.C. Wei said he was nervous about the scale of that investment, but after checking the financial status of the hyperscalers, he’s convinced the demand is permanent. Once again, I tend to trust the judgment of the CEO for years of flawless performance and access to the best sources.

Memory Shortage

For someone who's actively following memory shortage and invests in Micron (MU), I have to mention TSMC's main role in this story. As the industry has moved to HBM's (High Bandwidth Memory), companies rush to improve this chip (it's more like a system of multiple layers of silicon connected) to make it more powerful. As a result, the new 2026 standard is HBM4. This technology requires a sophisticated logic layer (base die, which acts as a communicator between the memory and the GPU to control the traffic). Memory companies aren't great at building these high-performance logic layers. Micron, for example, tried and failed because it requires precision, which DRAM manufacturing equipment can't provide. Consequently, they’ve had to stop trying to do it themselves and are now hiring TSMC to build it. Without TSMC’s high-end logic nodes, the memory from Micron or SK Hynix won't work in an AI data center.

Q1 2026

For the upcoming quarter, most analysts are looking for revenue to be at $35.24 billion, as per management's guidance, with an earnings per share estimate of around $3.31. They’ll probably report that their factories are running at near-total capacity, which is why their profit margins are expected to stay strong, likely at around 63%.

An interesting layer here is the war in the Middle East, though it might show up more in their future guidance than in this quarter. The conflict has made essentials like energy and raw materials more expensive and complicated to get, and it may impact the number of TSM as well, since Taiwan imports almost all of its energy. There's also a helium shortage from Qatar and bromine from Israel, which are critical for the lithography and manufacturing processes. Despite these, the report will likely still be a beat because the shortage began in the middle of March, at the very end of Q1. C.C.

At the same time, I got a very fascinating chart from my colleague, a Seeking Alpha analyst, Jack Bowman (thanks, Jack!). It shows that AI GPU availability has cratered to zero percent in March and April 2026. Both Jack and I believe it's due to a mix of factors related to the Middle East. Iran has made an extra effort targeting data centers, which, most likely, partly took the availability down but also scared the market and made it "lock in" the capacity while it is still there. If the trend continues, the difficulties with the supply chain may be fully offset by an additional surge in demand.

Valuation, Risks, and Conclusion

To understand the valuation of TSM, you have to know that when looking at companies in cyclical industries like semiconductors, the numbers always look amazing until the cycle reaches its peak. It’s not just that they’re selling more chips, but their profit margins, cash flow, and overall efficiency all increase, and the business looks perfect. As a result, the stock could look way cheaper than it actually is because the earnings are huge. If the demand starts decreasing, it would result not only in a revenue drop but also in a cash flow decrease, a margin drop, and all of the metrics will follow the same chain reaction. This is especially risky right now because TSM is spending a lot of money building out new factories. Together with the China and Taiwan risks, the stock should trade at a discount.

Even though the business is doing amazingly, the stock is not cheap. The best way, in my opinion, to judge the price is to compare it to its own five-year average on the valuation multiples. Right now, the price-to-earnings ratio is about 30% higher than its five-year average, and it means that the stock already trades at a premium due to the massive AI demand. While they deserve to trade at a premium given their dominance, it also suggests that the explosive growth stage of the stock price is probably behind us.

If you like the business and want to hold it long term, this is a reasonable time to get in, but you shouldn't expect the price to suddenly surge dozens of percentage points. It’s a great company at a fair price, and I rate it as a Buy, as I believe that the current cycle will be more prolonged, and it still has plenty of room to continue.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TEXAS INSTRUMENTS: This Analog Giant Deserves A Place In Long-Term Portfolios
data.description:
Thesis

I still view Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) as one of the best semiconductor plays available today, and I think there are three things guiding that view: (1) Texas Instruments is about to begin to finally benefit from a multi-year, $30-billion manufacturing buildout that will lead to mega margins, (2) a unique opportunity to be a significant artificial intelligence infrastructure beneficiary, and (3) Texas Instruments has a track record of returning value to investors that few other tech companies can come close to, in the form of 22 years of dividend increases. At close to $215 per share, I think TXN now is a stock that is just coming out of an investment-heavy phase, into a decade of margin expansion and FCF acceleration.

Company Overview

Despite what you might have heard, most people still think of Texas Instruments as a company that makes calculators for the classroom and it’s one of the most recognizable names in consumer electronics. But today, that’s not who they are at all. Texas Instruments is now the world’s largest maker of analog chips — unsexy yet absolutely essential semiconductors that convert signals from the real world into data large digital brains can use. They’re in cars, factory robots, medical devices, cell phones, and increasingly, the power systems in the largest data centers.

Founded in 1951 and based in Dallas, the company has a long history of innovation: first commercial silicon transistor in 1954, the handheld calculator in 1967, and the first microcontroller in 1970. In the decades since, Texas Instruments has purposefully gone after a narrower set of markets that are characterized by long product life cycles, a large customer base, and sticky demand: analog and embedded processing. Its focus is one of the things I like most; while others are competing in the newest nodes and try to win in AI design wins, Texas Instruments has created a moat with process efficiency, manufacturing control, and the staggering breadth of its catalog (it sells more than 80,000 products to some 100,000 customers globally).

Financial and Competitive Analysis

The numbers tell of a company coming out of what had been a purposefully managed spending cycle. For 2025, Texas Instruments booked $17.7 billion in revenue, a 13% increase year over year, while delivering earnings per share of $5.45, a climb of nearly 5%. What I find more compelling than this is the trend in free cash flow: $2.9 billion for 2025, a 96% rise from a year earlier. That was achieved even after accounting for capital investments of $4.7 billion. To me, this signifies serious operating leverage being created by the business even while it was still in the investment phase.

The heart of the investment thesis has, of course, been the new 300mm fabrication in Sherman, Texas, which came online in December. Texas Instruments has invested approximately $30 billion into this project, and I believe it is a truly game-changing initiative. The 300mm wafers created at the site produce 40% more chips than the average 200mm wafer (and what is industry standard now), which leads to reduced cost on a per-unit basis. Management announced they hoped to have 95% of their chips made in-house by the end of the decade, compared to today’s. This vertical integration is one of its key points of differentiation. While most of its "fabless" competition contracts out to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Texas Instruments owns its supply chain and offers protection from both geopolitical risk and foundry bottleneck pricing.

Now that the heaviest part of the CapEx cycle is over management having recently narrowed its targeted yearly spend to between $2 billion and $5 billion, below the prior-year peak of $5 billion — I believe free cash flow should show significant improvement over the next 2–3 years. This view is consistent with that of certain sell-side analysts, like those at Stifel Nicolaus, who recently upgraded the stock on the premise that the shift from the CapEx phase to the ship-ahead operational phase will liberate massive margin levers. If revenues continue to increase in the high single-to-low double digits as new capacity comes on stream, gross margins can even revert to the mid-60% levels of pre-CapEx Texas Instruments.

The dividend profile only boosts my confidence. With a yield of 2.9%, 22 years of consecutive hikes, 4% increase declared in 2025, 273% dividend growth over the past 10 years — it is a company that views shareholders as partners of the business. Moreover, in years of cash flush-outs, the management team never pulls the emergency stop or, even worse, cut the DPS, which reflects the capital allocation culture is deeply rooted.

Key Competitors

A discussion of Texas Instruments is not complete without touching on the competitive environment. My three competitors that I keep the closest eye on are Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), NXP Semiconductors N.V. (NXPI), and Infineon Technologies AG (IFNNY). Analog Devices is the most direct pure-play competitor to Texas Instruments in the analog space. Its portfolio is strong in signal processing and data conversion, lacking only Texas Instruments’ manufacturing scale and overall vertical integration. NXP is a fierce competitor in automotive semiconductors (a space Texas Instruments also remains aggressive in) and also has exposure to electric vehicles and advanced driver-assistance systems, making it relevant for comparison. Infineon is a leader in power semiconductors for automotive and industrial use, and being a German company operating out of the EU also means it has a different geopolitical risk profile. I believe what gives Texas Instruments an advantage over all three is its breadth of its catalog, ownership of manufacturing facilities, and cost benefits yielded from the 300mm transition, all of which snowball over time and are exceedingly difficult to imitate.

Catalysts and Recent News

Several developments caught my attention recently. On the positive side, CEO Haviv Ilan disclosed during the fourth-quarter earnings call that data center sales surged 70% year over year. This data point is very important because it shows that analog chips, especially power management and signal-chain chips, are fundamental to AI server infrastructure. Every GPU cluster requires sophisticated power delivery, and Texas Instruments is in a position to provide it. The AI tailwind for this company is real even if it doesn’t make headlines the way NVIDIA Corporation’s (NVDA) data center revenue does.

Government policy is a factor I am also keeping an eye on. Reports indicate that the Trump White House has added to the existing December 2025 launch of “Pax Silica” by pitching $4 trillion to create a voluntary investment consortium to pay for semiconductor supply chains, energy infrastructure and critical minerals. That’s not a typo - a $250 million initial federal contribution may sound paltry in comparison, but the message is clear: Washington wants an American high tech manufacturing renaissance. The improved Advanced Manufacturing Investment Tax Credits (“AMITC”) hidden inside the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” just sweetens the economics for Texas Instruments, which is building fabs on US soil. These policy tailwinds alleviate the investment dangers of the Sherman plant.

On the less favorable side, I note that Senior Vice President Hagop Kozanian announced plans to retire in August 2026. While executive departures always introduce some uncertainty, this appears to be a planned succession rather than an abrupt exit, so I do not assign it significant weight. More substantively, Texas Instruments has been raising analog chip prices to offset higher input costs, which is a double-edged sword: it supports margins in the near term but could dampen demand if customers face budget pressure in a potential economic slowdown.

Valuation

This is where I will try to be the most disciplined. The consensus narrative estimates that TI will generate ~$22.3 billion in revenue and ~$7.9 billion in earnings in 2028, translating to roughly ~10% YoY revenue growth (a healthy step-up from the current $17.7 billion base) and a meaningful gain in earnings. Using a DCF with these assumptions yields a fair value of approximately $219 per share, or about 10% above the current price of ~$215. Not a screaming bargain by any means, but I think the risk-reward is pretty good when you consider the dividend and the increased likelihood of higher estimates given the new fab ramp.

More bullish analysts pencil down the road toward revenues of almost $28.5 billion and earnings exceeding $10 billion. On the other hand, more conservative scenarios — such as if weaker end-market demand or a slower fab utilization throughput than factored into current predictions— can actually have a valuation 15–19% below today’s. My estimate aligns with the consensus median, reflecting the reasonableness of both arguments.

Risks

Three risks could alter my perspective. The first would be a meaningful slowdown in global industrial and auto markets (Texas Instruments’ two most important end markets), in which case the new fabrication would be oversupplied, hurting margins rather than improving them. Second would be an escalation of geopolitical tensions or a reversal in the US’s semiconductors policy, in which case the fundamental premise of building domestic manufacturing plants would become unsustainable. The third would be a cyclical downturn in the analog chips market, which would be characterized by an inventory correction and halt revenue growth for several quarters even if the company is in a strong competitive position.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Texas Instruments is one of the best places to invest in semiconductors without betting on any single particular tech cycle. The company’s analog tilt exposes it to just about every end-market in electronics; the 300mm buildout is delivering structural cost benefits that will continue to advance as demand persists; and the AI data center opportunity is adding an unexpected new growth driver to an already steady compounder. With the heaviest CapEx cycle in its history now completed, free cash flow looks set to turn higher, providing further fuel for a strong dividend and additional share price appreciation potential. While the stock isn’t particularly "cheap" at around $215, I think the durable business quality and clarity of the margin expansion story make Texas Instruments worth owning for a multi-year investor, and I would look to buy on any meaningful correction.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Northwest Bancshares Is Turning This Ship Around (Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in January of this year, I decided to downgrade shares of Northwest Bancshares (NWBI) from a "Hold" to a "Sell." This decision was based on weak asset quality and a high price-to-earnings multiple for the business. Since then, however, the market has disagreed with my view. Shares are up 8.8%, while the S&P 500 is down 3.8%. This is disappointing to me, to say the least. After all, I view a "Sell" candidate as one that should underperform the market for the foreseeable future. I can also understand, however, why the picture for the business has changed according to market participants.

The final quarter of 2025 was surprisingly robust, and asset quality for the company is improving. In fact, I would say that the picture is strong enough now that I can say that downgrading it, even though it was based on the most recent data available and my interpretation of where things were heading, was premature. For that reason, I think that upgrading it back to a "Hold" is the appropriate course of action.

Time to Backtrack

We really have not seen much additional data come out from Northwest Bancshares since I last wrote about the business. The only new data that we have access to covers the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. And honestly, it was a good time for the business. Take the balance sheet as an example. From 2024 to 2025, deposits for the institution expanded from $12.14 billion to $13.94 billion. What's really impressive here is that only $193 million of these deposits were high-cost broker deposits. That means the rest of them are organic in nature. What's more, 14% of the company's deposits, about $2 billion in total, were uninsured. This is quite a bit below the 30% maximum that I typically like to see.

Other aspects of the balance sheet have also improved. The company went from having $11.06 billion of loans to having $12.86 billion worth. This loan portfolio is pretty diverse in nature. Admittedly, there are certain areas of concentration. This includes 23.8% that's tied up in residential mortgage loans, 22.4% in commercial real estate, 19.5% of commercial loans, and 18.7% of vehicle loans.

To be honest with you, the vehicle loan situation is a bit concerning to me. After all, in the final quarter of 2025, auto loan delinquency rates were the highest they had been since the final quarter of 2024. That would be about 5.2%. And with economic conditions deteriorating, we can expect that trend to worsen. One other area that should be watched carefully would be exposure to office assets. 7.5% of the company’s commercial real estate portfolio was comprised of non-owner-occupied commercial office buildings. This does not even include another 3.2% of owner-occupied commercial office buildings. With rather high vacancy rates right now, this does create some risk. But I wouldn't call it high considering that it accounts for a relatively small portion of the overall loan portfolio.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve our attention as well. Take securities as an example. The company ended 2025 with $2.27 billion of securities on its books. That represented a nice increase over the $1.86 billion reported for 2024. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents did drop from $288.4 million to $233.6 million. And debt rose from $444.7 million to $691.2 million. But on the whole, this slightly higher leverage has paid off for the business.

As interest rates drop, banks are being given cover to slash how much they are paying depositors. For instance, for the final quarter of 2025, interest-bearing deposits cost the company just 1.97% per year. That compares to the 2.14% reported a year earlier. At the same time as this, management has been successful in issuing loans with higher interest rates. Those went from 5.56% per year to 5.65%. Investment securities, meanwhile, went from yielding 2.38% to 3.03%, while mortgage-backed securities increased from 2.60% to 2.97%. As these interest rates have changed, management has seen its net interest margin climb. It went from 3.42% in the final quarter of 2024 to 3.69% at the same time in 2025. And for the year as a whole, the 3.69% that the company reported well exceeded the 3.26% reported the year before that.

The chart above shows what impact this has had on revenue and profitability for the company. Even as non-interest income has been a bit lumpy, net interest income has increased materially. The end result here has been an increase in overall net profits. The $45.7 million in net income generated for the final quarter comfortably exceeded the $32.8 million reported a year earlier. And for the year as a whole, net profits jumped from $100.3 million to $126 million.

If we use the results achieved for the 2025 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced relative to earnings. Even with the nice improvement that the company exhibited, the firm is still trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 14.9. That is higher than any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart, and it is above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. The chart below shows something a bit different, though. On both a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis, the company is actually near the lower end of the spectrum. In fact, it's trading exactly at book value, which is quite appealing.

The improvements that the company has seen have also pushed up overall asset quality. When I last wrote about the company, return on assets was only 0.72%. But that number has risen to 1.10%, which is above the 1% minimum that I typically look for. It also puts the company above three of the five institutions that I compared it to in the first chart below. Return on equity, however, is an area where the institution is still unfortunately lagging. In the second chart below, you can see a reading of 9.70%, putting the company below all but one of the five banks that I compared it to. It also happens to be lower than the 10% minimum that I prefer.

Lastly, we have credit quality. This is an area that we have seen some deterioration in as of late. At the end of 2025, the firm reported that 0.83% of loans and 0.64% of assets were classified as non-performing. Both of these are worse off than the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see, and they're worse off than the 0.56% and 0.54%, respectively, that the company reported a year earlier. This should definitely be monitored by investors moving forward.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that I was premature in downgrading Northwest Bancshares. Looking at the newest data available, it's clear that management has done a fine job of improving operations in such a short window of time. I would make the case that while the stock is certainly far from deserving a bullish outlook, a more neutral one with a "Hold" rating is justified here.

data.title: Leggett & Platt: The Somnigroup Merger Is A Decent Exit, But No Bargain
data.description:
Leggett & Platt, Incorporated (LEG) is having a very positive day, with the stock surging 12% after the confirmation of the deal with Somnigroup International Inc. (SGI). What impressed me is that even with this important trigger, the stock price is still below what it was in my last article, that is, it really proved to be a Cigar Butt that did not offer “enough puffs.”

But what matters is that now the analysis changes completely. We are no longer talking about Leggett & Platt as a standalone company, but rather about the merger that should happen in the next quarters. What we really need to analyze, in fact, is the final company of this M&A, considering that now Somnigroup and Leggett finally “shook hands.”

A Word About the Deal

Detailing a bit more, the all-stock transaction is valued at $2.5 billion. The ratio is ~0.145 SGI shares for each LEG share. That is why Leggett is being traded for about $11.15 now, a small discount over the value of ~$11.50 that is found through the SGI stock that is at $79.1.

The expectation is that the transaction should be closed until the end of this year (2026), after the necessary approvals: LEG’s shareholders and also from other regulatory bodies. Because of that, there is a slight discount - the opportunity cost until the closing of 2026, the uncertainties of this deal really occurring without any impediment, and of course, the uncertainty of the price of the SGI stock until then.

But even with this complexity, the structure and incentives of the deal are simple. The SGI shareholders will be diluted, and the shareholders of LEG after the deal is concluded will have a stake in Somnigroup. And as this was already a long-lasting partnership, more specifically, about 50 years of collaboration, the intention of the deal is also a bit obvious. Somnigroup wants to internalize this old supplier, because it thinks it is at an interesting value, and sees a lot of potential for synergies.

At first, it seems to me to be something accretive for both. The LEG shareholder now no longer has the uncertainties and high dependence that it had on a deep value case and quite risky, and the SGI shareholder now has a new trigger of efficiency to be captured in the next years.

New Company, New Moats, New Triggers

In this type of operation, maybe the Leggett & Platt shareholder may want to make a short term trade to try to capture some additional upside or downside, for instance, try to arbitrate some potential of the deal being revised upward, it is possible to draw a parallel with what happened with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) in the last quarters. But particularly, I do not think that this is the main point of the question.

Both for the Leggett & Platt shareholder and for the SGI shareholder, I think that the ideal is to try to understand what are the new risks and new potentials of this new company, and then try to understand if it makes sense or not to continue being shareholder. E.g., if the conclusion of the shareholder is that there is still a relevant potential and a value to be captured, either through upside or shareholder yield, good, keep the shares, if you think it is something bad, exit the operation.

There are synergies to be captured, but there is also a component of moat increase for Somnigroup. This is because, for ~50 years these two companies were partners, and now Somnigroup brings all of this in-house. Now, Somnigroup will be the owner of the technology, there is an intangible gain here of being the owner of the innovation and being able to use this as a proprietary engine for other premium brands in general.

But besides this intangible gain, there are tangible gains which were what I mentioned about synergy. In the first 12 months there is already an expectation of ~$10 million of EBITDA increase for Leggett & Platt, further ahead capturing more $40 million, totaling $50 million in synergies. A part of this is sourcing and operations, improving supply integration and increasing logistics and manufacturing efficiency through scale and similar.

Another part is through innovation, which even though it is the smaller part in financial terms, it is something interesting to think about. As Somnigroup is a large company, there is an important component of expertise of engineering, design, and volume of data that Leggett & Platt will be able to use to optimize its products and serve better the final consumer.

And well, I know that $50 million seems little for a deal of this size, mainly thinking as the SGI shareholder. But we have to think that the whole operation becomes more “smooth.” There is an increase of verticalization. The friction decreases, so Somnigroup manages to control inventory issues more effectively, same for supply chain, and give a bigger match between demand and production.

So I believe that there is a stronger strategic component, more than only financial synergies. An interesting point that they raised in the presentation was the increase of the total addressable market (TAM). Somnigroup today is a 100% bedding company (looking to its product mix). The combined company will have 12% of top line coming from furniture, flooring and textile, and 10% coming from specialized products.

By the way, in the bedding market itself there is a considerable expansion. Somnigroup cites other geographies, that’s why the “geographic mix” increases in the international segment, there is also an entire consolidated operation in foam and adjustable bed, this all increases the diversification and verticalization of Somnigroup.

New Risks For The Combined Company

Ok, so until now we have that this combined company will capture some synergies, expand moat, partnerships, and addressable market. Summarizing, it will become a better, more complete company.

But it also changes the risk profile.

Somnigroup was already a case that had synergies to be captured by other acquisitions like the one of Mattress Firm, and it is something that even improves a bit the track-record of Somnigroup, it has already done large acquisitions before. But at the same time, it increases the integration risk.

M&A is always very simple on paper, but in practice it is another story.

There is always some level of uncertainty, something that was supposed to be simple execution and in fact turned out different from planned. Some contracts that would be easily revised and now there is a fine, some cost that would “disappear” because it was redundant and did not come out as the management expected, some systems that were not of easy integration and so on. Even more intangible things like culture, can put an additional challenge in this.

But for the question of Leggett & Platt there is a more specific challenge. Today, LEG is a "neutral" company, right? Just as it was an important supplier for Somnigroup, it is also the supplier of other companies. These companies are competitors of Somnigroup.

If you are a Somnigroup competitor, will you continue buying from Leggett & Platt post-merge?

I am not saying that the customers will stop buying, I am just raising an uncertainty. Somnigroup will maintain Leggett & Platt operating in a separate way and this mitigates a bit the risks, but maybe there is a more negative perception by the customers, maybe a certain resistance of some to maintain the purchase volume of a company that now was acquired by an important rival.

If this really is an uncertainty that materializes, maybe we'll have a scenario where there is a margin expansion by the captured synergies, but a lower revenue.

Bottom Line: An Optimistic Stance, But Not That Much

From a Leggett & Platt shareholder perspective, the deal seems minimally interesting to me. There is not a substantial premium, the acquisition multiple is still small, but at least it is a decent “exit.” And for those who intend to maintain the shares of Somnigroup, it is a decent combined company.

Ex-Leggett & Platt acquisition, SGI was already an interesting company. A strong brand power, vertical integration and a lot of scale. With the acquisition of LEG, this only increases. One more company in the portfolio, more technology, more scale, more margin expansion to come in the next few years.

But particularly, it is not a case that catches my attention currently. The combined company will have ~$11.2 billion of top line and ~$1.7 billion of adj. EBITDA. Today SGI is a company of $23 billion of enterprise value, and with the acquisition the enterprise value will increase some hundreds of millions with the debt of Leggett & Platt; as well as with the issuance of shares it may increase a bit more (~$2.5 billion in new shares). With the current EV, the implied EV-to-EBITDA is 13.5x, potentially higher at the end of the acquisition due to the factors mentioned.

Even with the positive prospects of margin increase and an eventual better cycle for this niche (which would increase the top line), it does not seem like a bargain to me. And yes, the expected growth is high here (see the table below). In fact, that’s another reason I’m a bit more skeptical. The market is already pricing in an EPS CAGR of +20% for the coming years, so if any uncertainties materialize and SGI fails to deliver on that, the stock could see some de-rating.

So even though Somnigroup is an interesting company and that is becoming stronger, I do not think that it is worth a buy rating here.

Because of that, my rating is a Hold for Leggett & Platt and for Somnigroup.

data.title: Prices Are Going Higher
data.description:
"Consumer prices soared in March, pushed higher by skyrocketing gasoline prices."

This quote is from Konrad Putzier in the Wall Street Journal.

Overall inflation, year-over-year, came in at 3.3 percent in March, up from 2.4 percent, year-over-year, in February.

"Core" inflation rose 2.6 percent in March, down from 2.7 percent in February.

Energy prices...up 12.5 percent, year-over-year, in March.

They were up 0.5 percent in February.

Gasoline prices rose 18.9 percent year-over-year.

Fuel oil jumped 44.2 percent.

Analysts have been saying that inflation is going to return.

Well, it looks as if inflation has returned.

Or, we all should be looking out for the reality that inflation has returned.

The thing is that Trump and his team have "messed around" with so many things, tariffs and all, that there are a lot of places that seem to be showing the outbreak of inflation once again.

But the full experience of a return to inflation will only be confirmed with a sustained rise in prices.

Mr. Putzier writes that

"If energy prices remain high, that could filter through to higher prices for food--fertilizer is made from natural gas--and other goods."

Putzier quotes Lindsey Piegza, chief economist at Stifel:

"We won't feel the bulk of that for perhaps a month or two."

And then there is the question mark about what is going to happen to monetary policy.

In the last couple of months, the Federal Reserve has moved back into a stance of quantitative easing.

Since early December 2025, the Fed has begun to add securities to its securities held outright. The increase has been pretty aggressive, especially since Trump began his little "battle" with Iran. The "war" began on February 28, 2026, and more purchases followed. This is the result that the President seemed to want.

It seems that the stock market has gotten with these moves and started to move up again in the past week.

This is a needed component of the future health of consumer spending and economic growth.

Lydia DePillis, writing in the New York Times, emphasizes this reliance upon "wealth effects" driving greater consumer spending...which results in faster economic growth.

DePillis explains how the wealth effect works. It comes from "the free-spending mentality among higher-income earners fostered by the ballooning value of investment portfolios and retirement accounts in recent years."

The wealth generated by higher stock prices means greater consumer wealth, and this leads to greater spending on consumer goods and services...followed by..."overall" GDP growth!

This has been the picture of "how things work" that is built into the Federal Reserve's picture of how monetary policy can guide the economy.

And, for the past seventeen years or so...the Federal Reserve seems to have been very successful in seeing this narrative work out.

So, DePillis provides a picture of where the Federal Reserve appears to be going as it expands its securities portfolio.

And here is the picture of U.S. economic growth, year-over-year, going back to the Great Recession in 2009.

The point I am trying to get to is that the Federal Reserve is in a position to keep the U.S. economy expanding and expanding at a rate of growth that is in excess of 2.0 percent.

The growth rate of the M2 money stock is now around 4.4 percent, year-over-year.

The M2 velocity of circulation appears to be relatively constant over the past year or so, but here is where all of the disruptions surrounding tariffs and other policy uncertainties can cloud up the picture.

The disruptions being created will mostly impact the velocity of circulation of the money stock. So, this is where the greatest amount of "unknown" will come from.

And, in these areas, experts are expecting the most action...the most impact on the final results from the "fiscal" side of the equation.

That is, the experts are expecting that the velocity of circulation of the money stock will impact the results.

And the U.S. economy may give us an economic growth within the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent range; the rate of inflation is expected to be closer to what was produced in March...or even higher.

The crucial thing, as Mr. Putzier points out, is that these actions need to work themselves out. This will take some time.

So, investors need to stay alert.

Everything...now...seems to be working to push inflation to a higher rate. Even the Federal Reserve seems to have moved in this direction.

And, if the Federal Reserve has moved in this direction....

data.title: Anthropic Mythos Or Not, Software And Cybersecurity Are Still Uninvevstable
data.description:
The selloff in software is a painful lesson for contrarian investors to never underestimate the power of irrational pessimism.

What selloff?

Today, software is outperforming any other industry in tech.

I'm talking about the decline since October 2025, with the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) and the Global X Cybersecurity ETF (BUG), down approximately 35%, mostly on what I think are irrational fears about the AI disruption in the industry.

Yes, I said the word irrational, but I have a hold rating on the IGV and BUG ETFs.

Some readers may be confused at this point. I mean, there is a clear disconnection between price action and fundamentals, right? Isn't that a mispricing opportunity that can be exploited?

All of these are valid questions. In fact, as I'm about to discuss in this piece, I strongly believe the AI disruption fear is completely irrational.

However, that doesn't mean that the lows are in. In fact, the downside catalysts after the release of a new LLM model, like the Mythos this week, make the long-term setup very fragile.

Let me be crystal clear: AI is not removing the real bottleneck in software, in my view. Building the product is only step zero. The hard part is distribution, user acquisition, and access to capital and an audience. AI is not useful for the latter as much as it is for writing code.

On top of that, the Mythos is likely going to drive up the cybersecurity budget of large corporations in the U.S., which is a tailwind for cybersecurity stocks.

However, regardless of whether the disruption thesis is true in practice or not, perception drives price more than operating reality (i.e., fundamentals). At this point, swimming against the current makes little sense to me. That's why I'm staying on the sidelines.

A Fragile Setup

In my view, this industry is the most fragile theme to own in one's portfolio, even beyond private credit.

It has nothing — let me be crystal clear — absolutely nothing to do with fundamentals or any other rational argument.

As I wrote many times before, the AI disruption fear appears irrational to anyone with hands-on experience in the software space.

How would I know?

Well, I own Guidance Terminal. If you've ever visited my site, how many visits do you think I get each day?

  • 1,000?
  • 10,000?
  • 100,000?

Please take a look at the analytics page showing the visits in the last 7 days.

I hope I'm making myself very clear: having a product in hand and being successful "ain't the same ting", as Fat Tony (from Taleb's Antifragile book) would say.

In the last 5-6 years, I developed many software tools in many niches, and the results I got were similar to the one above.

Why?

Because having the software in hand is just step 0.

The real work begins with the user acquisition phase. That's when founders need deep pockets (most of the time from naive VCs or private capital firms) and key connections with those who own the audience.

Guess what? AI is not going to help these founders with these two items, and user acquisition is, by far, the most important part of the software business journey.

Add on top of that obstacles like the headline below, and the vibecoding crowd has a slim chance (at best) to displace the incumbents.

Bottom line: videcoding as a tool, even when orders of magnitude superior to the incumbents, is far (and I really mean far) from taking their pie.

That's just my first argument on why the AI disruption fear is irrational.

So, why were software and IT services down in the last week, when every other industry in tech is in the green after the ceasefire in the Middle East?

Simply, because investors ain't developers or software founders with hands-on experience. That's why headlines like the one below make them panic sell first, and ask questions (if any) later.

The reason I say software is fragile is that as soon as the big two LLM startups, namely OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) (sorry, Gemini and Grok), release a new model, the stocks in software take an instant hit.

Therefore, the amount of downside catalysts ahead is significant, and given the fact that each LLM model is better than the last, each hit on the IGV (software ETF) may be harder than the last.

Anthropic's Mythos Model

This week, the main headline in the AI space was the release (not to the general public, though) of the Claude Mythos Preview model. Specifically, the company introduced Project Glasswing. I include an interesting excerpt below from the press release:

We're partnering with the organizations responsible for the infrastructure billions of people depend on, and giving their defenders a head start with our newest frontier model, Claude Mythos Preview. The initiative brings together Amazon Web Services, Anthropic, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorganChase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks as launch partners.

According to Anthropic, the Mythos model has already identified thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across critical infrastructure. Naturally, this release brought government attention to this tool.

According to some sources, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell convened an urgent meeting with major bank CEOs to warn them about cyber risks posed by Mythos.

Naturally, this led to another selloff in software and cybersecurity stocks while the broader tech sector remained intact.

Therefore, the note that Wedbush recently published on Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) after the software selloff may be completely accurate (in my view), but it's missing the point that the market doesn't care if "Anthropic is eating Palantir's lunch" is a fictional narrative or not.

We acknowledge the growth from Anthropic is unprecedented, but we believe the take that Anthropic is eating PLTR's lunch (most recently amplified by Michael Burry's now-deleted post on X earlier today) is the wrong take and fictional narrative (in our view), as Palantir is at the epicenter of leaders in the AI Revolution while its AIP product moat remains unmatched in our view," Wedbush noted. "The data-driven moat by Palantir is around the data and its ontology…this is NOT being disrupted by Claude…if anything, it's accelerated on the enterprise.

The IGV and BUG ETFs

These are my two favourite ETFs to track the software and cybersecurity industries.

Let's start with the IGV. This is commonly known as the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF, or software ETF. It's not the only one, but it's the one that is referred to most of the time when talking about software.

This ETF provides a targeted exposure to North American software companies, with a small slice of interactive home entertainment and interactive media names:

Its expense ratio of 0.39% is quite attractive, considering that the fund had 112 holdings as of April 10.

As of the latest update, the ETF had $10.13 billion in net assets and an average 30-day volume of 21.9 million at a spread of 0.01%. I include below the top 10 names, which altogether make up 62% of the portfolio.

This is not your everyday income passive fund. The IGV is a pure growth ETF, with a 3-year beta of 1.23. Naturally, one cannot expect a cheap P/E multiple given that it mainly tracks growth stocks. As of April 10, the average P/E ratio is 30.8x, and the average P/B is 5.6x.

From a pure price action perspective, this ETF is underwater in terms of 1-year returns:

That's why it's attractive to a decent amount of contrarians, based on the Seeking Alpha articles (and comments) page on this ETF in the last few months.

Moving on to the BUG ETF (as an amateur dev, I really love the ticker). The official name of this ETF is the Global X Cybersecurity ETF. This is another thematic passive ETF that aims to track the broader Indxx Cybersecurity Index.

Given the overlap that some cybersecurity companies have with software, the index requires a company to generate at least 50% of its revenue from cybersecurity activities to qualify as a buying candidate.

In terms of constituents, it's smaller than the IGV, with only 30 stocks and about $770 million in net assets. I include below the top-10 holdings, which add up to 63.52% of the fund:

Speaking of the constituents, 79% of them are based in the U.S., followed by Israel at 8.6% and Japan at 5.8%.

From an expense perspective, this fund has a 0.50% fee, higher than the IGV ETF.

What I found interesting from a price action perspective is the beta between the two ETFs.

Even though software and cybersecurity aren't the same thing, it appears that the stocks within these two industries have an apparent correlation. That's why, if I am neutral on software, I am therefore neutral on cybersecurity as well.

Looking Ahead

Based on the arguments provided above, I remain neutral on the software and cybersecurity industry as I simply don't see why any of the constituents in this pocket of the market will turn around the pessimism that surrounds their stocks.

As I argued in the last earnings season, fundamentals and growth are not enough for a turnaround in software. Therefore, I prefer to take the current pessimism seriously (despite the fact that it's grounded in an unrealistic pillar) and allocate my capital elsewhere.
